texts.orig: ABSTRACT
texts.text: ABSTRACT

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We try to approach a known equivalence to RH involving relative maxima and minima of ξ ( s ) on critical line by a representation of the derivative of the phase of ξ ( s ) with respect to imaginary coordinate that involves directly Euler product. In this attempt it is found an object conjectured to be , almost everywhere, the converging 'spectrum' of prime numbers. Reasons and consequences of the conjecture are highlighted.
texts.text: We try to approach a known equivalence to RH involving relative maxima and minima of ξ ( s ) on critical line by a representation of the derivative of the phase of ξ ( s ) with respect to imaginary coordinate that involves directly Euler product. In this attempt it is found an object conjectured to be , almost everywhere, the converging 'spectrum' of prime numbers. Reasons and consequences of the conjecture are highlighted.

texts.orig: · Right and left term of (3.2) converge, excluding singular points, at least for ϵ > -1 / 2. Expression (3.2) comes from right term of (3.1) upon subtracting ln ( 1 ( s -1) ) . Following tab.1 (third row) it produces an Li ( x ) term that becomes (3.15) after incremental ratio (3.11) processing. Instead ln( ζ ( s )), after same elaboration, becomes (3.13). Both are diverging for ϵ < 1 / 2 if taken alone. But taken together?. For (3.2) their algebric sum seems to tend to ∂ ℑ [ln( ζ ( s )( s -1))] ∂t , as if computed from (3.2) at least for ϵ > -| δ | > -1 / 2. Otherwise we would reach conflicting results only following different paths of permissible calculations, i.e derivative along t of phase of (3.2), and, (3.21). This could be perhaps a proof, but, is better to be cautious.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Another observation in (3.21) computations at ϵ = 0. Correlation peaks start to appear, even if at low resolution, with relatively low p max and p ∗ . Afterward, as p max and p ∗ increase, they persist where a zero is present, They increase the height, and lower the width, but, they remain in place for following increased p max and p ∗ . We never saw higher p max and p ∗ results that contrast with previous ones. It seems as if relatively big primes confirms always resonances just arisen with relatively small primes.

texts.orig: We found an expression (3.22) that is a correlation between: (1) difference of prime powers distribution, J ( x ), from the mean Li ( x ), and, (2) a weighted cos(ln( x ) t ). It can be interpreted as the 'spectrum of primes'. In (3.22) formulation, the zeros of ζ ( s ) are the t -abscissa of correlation peaks of d [ J ( x ) -Li ( x )], with the weighted cos(ln( x ) t ), along x .

With ϵ -ϵ ∗ k ≤ 0 (see (4.1 ) ) we have that the peak at ϵ = ϵ ∗ k , t = t ∗ k becomes a ridge in the | t -t ∗ k | < 2 π ln( p ∗ ) region, like in ∂ ℑ [ln( ζ ( s )( s -1))] ∂t . This ridge shrinks, along t , as p ∗ →∞ even if it is prohibitive from a computational point of view. See fig. 3, and, fig 6. If conjecture, whose rationale is explained in subsection 3.1, is true, then this 'spectrum of primes' converges, at least, for p max ≥ p ∗ →∞ , at least in 0 ≤ ϵ ≤ 1 / 2 , ∀ t = t ∗ k .

texts.orig: Figure 6: Computation of (3.21 ) with p max = 60 × 10 6 and p ∗ = 6 × 10 6 with a sliding window of length W = 2 π ln( p max ) = 2 π ln(6 × 10 7 ) to counteract Gibbs Phenomenon. The shift on t axis is not compensated. Notice that, for | t -t ∗ k | > 2 π ln( p ∗ ) , curves with lower ϵ are below curve with higher ϵ as predicted by Lemma 3. If we put p max = p ∗ = 6 × 10 6 we get a plot similar to this one, if then we do smoothing with W = 2 π ln( p max ) = 2 π ln(6 × 10 6 ) we get, practically, this same figure. Notice the greater positive peak for negative ϵ . It is due to the discontinuity of ℑ [ln( ζ ( s )( s -1))] along t = t ∗ k , ϵ ≤ ϵ ∗ k , here = 0 .
